about.title: About Us
about.subtitle: Car rental in Herceg Novi with Igalo, Bijela and airport pickup
about.seoDesc: Herceg Novi rentals made for outer-bay drives: fixed rates, insurance bundled in, Tivat or Dubrovnik airport collection, and local support from drivers who know the Orjen road.
about.p1: Car Rental Herceg Novi connects travellers with reliable rental cars in the outer Bay of Kotor. We work with trusted local operators to offer a range of vehicles, from compact city cars for the stepped lanes of the Old Town to 4x4s for the Orjen road up to Ledenice.
about.heading2: Why a Car Makes Sense in Herceg Novi
about.p2: Public transport around the outer bay is limited: buses follow the magistrala and skip the side roads entirely. The Kamenari ferry shortcut across the Verige strait, the day trip to Rose on Luštica, the border run to Dubrovnik 40 minutes west, and the switchback climb up Orjen all reward travellers with their own wheels. A rental car turns a two-day stop into a proper exploration of the outer bay and the mountains behind it.
about.p3a: We operate car rental services across
about.p3d: , and locations throughout the coast and interior.
about.promise1: Comprehensive cover with zero-excess upgrade available
about.promise2: Tivat or Dubrovnik airport meet-and-greet, your car waits at arrivals
about.promise3: Transparent pricing, the quoted figure is the final figure
about.promise4: Cross-border travel to Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia, and Kosovo
about.localText: Based in Herceg Novi, we know every stretch of road around the outer bay — the Kamenari ferry timetable, the Debeli Brijeg queue times, the Orjen switchbacks, and the parking tricks along Šetalište Pet Danica that guidebooks never mention. Whether you are heading from
about.localMid2: stepped Old Town, crossing on the Kamenari ferry to
about.localEnd: , or climbing the Orjen road toward Ledenice viewpoint, we can help you map out the ideal route.
about.contactText: Questions about renting a car in Herceg Novi? We are available by phone, WhatsApp, and Viber. Browse the fleet online and reserve in minutes, or drop us a message and we will help you find the right vehicle for your trip.

montenegro.seoDesc: Montenegro car rental based in Herceg Novi: unlimited km for inland trips to Durmitor, Skadar Lake and Ostrog, plus quick border runs to Dubrovnik. Full insurance, four-season tyres.

border-crossing.seoDesc: Cross-border rental from Herceg Novi: Croatia 25 km via Debeli Brijeg, Bosnia via Trebinje through the Sutorina valley, Albania via Sukobin. Full green card coverage.

fullInsurance.title: Comprehensive Cover
fullInsurance.desc: Every rental includes CDW and third-party liability. Upgrade to zero excess if the Orjen hairpins or the tight Old Town stepped lanes make you nervous.

support.title: Round-the-Clock Assistance
support.desc: Flat tyre above Ledenice? Missed the Kamenari ferry? Our local team answers day and night — they know every road around the outer bay.

noHiddenFees.title: Transparent Pricing
noHiddenFees.desc: The figure on your confirmation is the figure at the desk. No fuel surprises, no airport levies, no extras sneaked in at collection.

freeCancellation.title: Flexible Cancellation
freeCancellation.desc: Plans shift, ferries queue, Orjen roads close after snow. Cancel most bookings free up to a week before your collection date.

crossBorder.title: Cross-Border Ready
crossBorder.desc: Croatia via Debeli Brijeg 25 km west, Bosnia through Trebinje via the Sutorina valley, Albania via Sukobin — Green Card paperwork prepared at booking time.

0.text: We landed at Tivat Airport and drove straight to Rafailovići for sunset. The bay road was stunning, water on one side, cliffs on the other. Next morning we tackled the Lovcen serpentine. The car handled every hairpin perfectly. Seamless from start to finish.

1.text: Third time renting in Budva and the booking process keeps getting smoother. This time I drove south to Budva and up to Cetinje in one day. Having a car makes destinations like Njegos Mausoleum and Lake Skadar actually accessible instead of theoretical.

2.text: We arrived by cruise ship and needed a car for two days. Picked up right near the port, visited Rafailovići and Herceg Novi, then drove the coastal road to Dubrovnik. The cross-border paperwork was ready, no delays at all. Perfect family road trip.

0.q: What do I need to bring for pickup?
0.a: A valid driving licence, your passport or national ID, and a physical credit card in the lead driver's name. Licences without Latin characters also require an International Driving Permit.

1.q: Can I cross into Croatia or other countries?
1.a: Absolutely. Croatia is about two hours via the Debeli Brijeg checkpoint, we include the Green Card at booking. Bosnia, Albania, Serbia, and Kosovo are also available with advance notice.

2.q: What age do I need to be?
2.a: The minimum is 21 years old with at least two years of driving experience. Drivers under 25 pay a young-driver supplement, the exact amount depends on the vehicle category.

3.q: What does the rental price cover?
3.a: Every booking includes CDW insurance, third-party liability, VAT, and a roadside safety kit. Most vehicles offer unlimited mileage, check the individual listing for details.

4.q: How do I collect the car at Tivat Airport Airport?
4.a: A representative meets you inside the Tivat Airport arrivals area with a name board. Your car is in the terminal car park, steps away. The drive to Budva Old Town is around fifteen minutes.

5.a: Yes, pick up and drop off at different locations across 28+ points in Montenegro. A one-way surcharge may apply depending on the distance between locations.

6.q: What if I need to cancel?
6.a: Most vehicles offer free cancellation up to seven days before pickup. Premium and convertible classes require thirty days. A small processing fee applies to all cancellations.

7.a: Most cars require between €100 and €350 held on a credit card. Some economy vehicles are deposit-free, these are clearly marked in your search results.

8.a: Many vehicles include unlimited mileage. Those with limits charge a per-kilometre rate beyond the allowance, always visible before you confirm.

9.a: None. The displayed price includes every tax and fee. No airport surcharges, no fuel-policy add-ons, nothing extra at the counter.

10.q: How much is the booking deposit?
10.a: A 15-20% advance payment secures your reservation. The balance is settled directly with the rental agent when you arrive to collect the vehicle.

11.q: Can I rent without a credit card?
11.a: A handful of vehicles are available without a credit card requirement. Look for the no-deposit filter when searching by pickup location.

aboutBody.h1: Rental Cars Matched to Outer-Bay Roads
aboutBody.p1: Car Rental Herceg Novi partners with trusted local operators to offer a fleet suited to outer-bay conditions: compact hatchbacks for the stepped lanes of the Old Town and central Šetalište Pet Danica, SUVs for the Orjen road to Ledenice, and premium saloons for cross-border runs to Dubrovnik and Trebinje. Every vehicle is supplied by established operators around the bay, serviced regularly, and delivered with full insurance.
aboutBody.h2: Why a Car Changes Everything Here
aboutBody.p2: Scheduled buses follow the magistrala and stop. That is the extent of public transport. They will not detour to Rose on the Luštica peninsula, nor climb to Ledenice on Orjen at 700 m, nor reach the back road down to Žanjice. They cannot time a crossing on the Kamenari–Lepetane ferry or pause at the Savina monastery above Igalo. Only your own wheels and a willingness to explore unlock the outer bay properly.
aboutBody.p3: We offer collection across the outer bay: central Herceg Novi, Igalo, Bijela, Đenovići, Kumbor (Portonovi), Tivat Airport (40 minutes east) and Dubrovnik Airport (40 minutes west across the Croatian border). Economy hatchbacks thread through Old Town approach roads effortlessly. SUVs handle the Orjen switchbacks without breaking stride. Every vehicle carries full insurance, and cross-border documentation for Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia, and Kosovo is arranged at the time of booking.
aboutBody.promise1: Full insurance with a zero-excess upgrade on every reservation
aboutBody.promise2: A named representative at Tivat or Dubrovnik arrivals, sign in hand, vehicle parked outside
aboutBody.promise3: Transparent pricing, no airport levies, no fuel-policy surprises
aboutBody.promise4: Green Card paperwork arranged for five neighbouring countries
aboutBody.promise5: Free cancellation up to seven days before your collection date
aboutBody.promise6: Round-the-clock roadside support, from Herceg Novi to Žabljak and everywhere in between
aboutBody.p4: Ask us which mornings the Kamenari ferry queue builds, we can advise you to cross before 09:00 or loop inland via Trebinje instead. Ask about the Orjen trails and we can point you toward the Ledenice viewpoint and the Subra ridge. From the Debeli Brijeg checkpoint timings to the quietest hour for Žanjice beach, we draw on local knowledge from partners and drivers working across the outer bay.
aboutBody.p5: WhatsApp, Viber, a traditional phone call, whichever suits you. We respond quickly, even during the hectic July cruise-ship season. Browse the fleet online, lock in a rate, and we take care of the rest. Or message us a question about parking near the Cathedral, the best route to Cetinje, or whether that mountain pass is open in December.
aboutBody.h6: Our Vehicles and What They Handle
aboutBody.p6: Each car in the fleet is chosen for the realities of Montenegrin roads. The compact hatchbacks — Fiat Panda, VW Polo, Renault Clio — slot into the narrow lane along Marshal Tito in Herceg Novi where larger vehicles simply cannot fit. For families or longer trips, the mid-size SUVs (Dacia Duster, Nissan Qashqai) carry luggage comfortably and handle the loose gravel on the Orjen road without hesitation. Premium saloons suit the motorway run to Podgorica via Nikšić or the cross-border highway to Dubrovnik. Vehicles are serviced by our partner operators in Herceg Novi and Bijela, checked between rentals, and delivered with a full tank of fuel and verified tyre pressures for mountain driving.
aboutBody.h7: A Typical Week Behind the Wheel
aboutBody.p7: Monday morning: collect in central Herceg Novi, walk the 100,000 Steps, afternoon coffee on the waterfront. Tuesday: Kamenari ferry across the Verige strait, day in Perast with the Our Lady of the Rocks boat, loop back through Risan for the Roman mosaics. Wednesday: the Orjen road up to Ledenice for the outer-bay panorama, then down to Igalo for the mud-bath spa. Thursday: cross at Debeli Brijeg in the morning, spend the afternoon in Dubrovnik, back for dinner in the Old Town. Friday: Luštica loop to Rose and Žanjice, short ferry across to Mamula Island. Saturday: inland via the Sutorina valley to Trebinje for Bosnian lunch. Sunday: return the car. That entire circuit covers roughly 450 km. Fuel cost: approximately 45 to 55 euros at current diesel prices. No bus, tour group, or taxi could replicate it at any price.
aboutBody.p8: Summer brings the cruise ships calling at Kotor, up to four vessels per day between June and September, and with them passengers spilling out across the bay. Herceg Novi stays quieter — its harbour is too shallow for the big ships — but the Kamenari ferry queue and the Debeli Brijeg border both build from mid-morning. We advise an early start or a late-evening cross. Winter transforms the outer bay into a quieter place: fewer visitors, lower rates, and Orjen roads that occasionally close above 900 metres due to snow. Spring and autumn are the sweet spot. The Mimosa Festival in February paints Igalo yellow, September still offers warm seas without the crowds, and the Orjen road is open and uncrowded.

montenegroBody.h1: Driving Montenegro from a Budva Base
montenegroBody.p1: Montenegro is roughly the size of Northern Ireland yet contains a coast, a deep fjord-like bay, a 2,500-year-old walled town, a 1,300-metre-deep canyon, and alpine peaks above 2,500 metres. Driving distances look short on a map. In practice, the roads twist, climb, tunnel, and ferry-cross their way between these extremes, and journey times rarely match what GPS predicts. Accept that, and the driving itself becomes part of the holiday.
montenegroBody.p2: Budva sits at the geographical heart of the action. Rafailovići is fifteen minutes north. Budva is thirty minutes south. Lovcen's summit is an hour of switchbacks above. Lake Skadar is ninety minutes inland. Only Durmitor, in the far north, requires a genuine road trip. Everything else radiates outward from the fortress ramparts like spokes from a hub.
montenegroBody.startText: Tivat Airport Airport, 8 km from Budva, is the obvious collection point, fifteen minutes from landing to the Old Town. Podgorica Airport offers wider winter schedules and a scenic 90-minute drive through the mountains. Dubrovnik Airport in Croatia has the most international routes and connects to Budva via a two-hour coastal road with one border crossing. All three have meet-and-greet handovers.
montenegroBody.timesText1: Rafailovići: 15 min. Tivat Airport: 20 min. Budva: 30 min. Herceg Novi: 45 min. Cetinje: 60 min. Podgorica: 90 min. Dubrovnik: 2 h. Zabljak (Durmitor): 3.5-4 h.
montenegroBody.timesText2: The Budva, Lovcen serpentine alone contains 25 documented hairpin bends in less than 20 km. Navigation apps routinely underestimate mountain stretches by 20-30%. Plan accordingly and carry water.
montenegroBody.rulesIntro: Random roadside checks are common, especially on the Riviera road and near border crossings. Officers will ask for:
montenegroBody.rule1: A valid driving licence (international driving permit accepted alongside it)
montenegroBody.rule2: The original rental contract (photocopies are not sufficient)
montenegroBody.rule3: Proof of insurance (the rental company provides this in the vehicle)
montenegroBody.rule4: A Green Card if crossing any border (approximately 15 euros for 15 days)
montenegroBody.keyRule1: Seat belts: mandatory for every occupant, front and rear
montenegroBody.keyRule2: Mobile phones: hands-free only, and even then police may stop you
montenegroBody.keyRule3: Alcohol: zero-tolerance policy, any detectable blood alcohol means an on-the-spot fine or worse
montenegroBody.keyRule4: Speed cameras: fixed units on the Riviera road and mobile traps on the highway. Fines are issued to the rental company and charged back to your card
montenegroBody.conditionsTitle: What the Roads Are Actually Like
montenegroBody.conditionsText: The bay road between Budva and Rafailovići is paved and maintained but barely two cars wide in places, with stone walls on one side and the water on the other. Mountain roads above 800 metres may lack guard rails entirely. The Sozina tunnel to Budva is modern and well-lit. The Lovcen road is narrow, steep, and has limited passing places, honk before blind bends. In winter, chains or winter tyres are legally required above the snow line.
montenegroBody.e65Text: This route traces the entire Budva Riviera from Herceg Novi around the northern arm through Rafailovići and Budva, then south via Tivat Airport toward Budva. Alternatively, the Kamenari, Lepetane car ferry shortcuts the loop, saving 45 minutes. The bay road is where most visitors spend their driving time, and for good reason, the views change with every headland.
montenegroBody.e762Text: Cuts inland from the Adriatic through the Montenegrin heartland toward the Serbian and eastern Bosnian borders. This is the route to Niksic, the Piva canyon, and eventually Durmitor. Less scenic near the coast but increasingly dramatic as it climbs.
montenegroBody.borderText: Cross-border driving is permitted with a Green Card. The Croatia crossing at Debeli Brijeg is the busiest, in July and August, waits of one to two hours are normal. Weekday mornings before 08:00 or evenings after 20:00 cut the queue significantly. Other crossings (Albania, Bosnia, Serbia, Kosovo) are generally faster.
montenegroBody.nationText: Independent only since 2006, Montenegro remains lightly visited compared to Croatia or Greece. The roads are quieter, the parking easier, and the landscapes more concentrated. A single tank of fuel can take you from Budva's Venetian alleyways to a glacial mountain lake and back. Few countries this small pack this much into their borders.
montenegroBody.fuelP1: Montenegro has three main fuel brands: Jugopetrol (part of Hellenic Petroleum), Eko, and several independent stations. Prices are regulated and relatively uniform across the country at approximately 1.45 to 1.55 euros per litre for diesel and slightly more for petrol. All major stations accept credit cards, though smaller independent stations in the north occasionally require cash.
montenegroBody.fuelP2: On the coast, fuel stations are frequent, you will pass one every 15 to 20 km along the main highways. In the mountains, they thin out considerably. Fill your tank in Budva or Budva before heading to Lovcen, Cetinje, or anywhere in the interior. The Njeguski highland stretch and the road between Cetinje and Podgorica via the old mountain route have no fuel stops at all.
montenegroBody.speedP1: Built-up areas: 50 km/h. Open roads: 80 km/h. Dual carriageways and motorway-standard roads: 100 km/h (or 130 km/h where posted). The bay road fluctuates between 40 and 60 km/h depending on the settlement. Speed cameras are fixed at several points along the Riviera road, near Bečići, near Risan, and at the entrance to the Vrmac tunnel.
montenegroBody.speedP2: Mobile police speed traps appear randomly, particularly on the highway between Budva and Podgorica and on the approach to border crossings. Fines are issued on the spot and range from 30 to 200 euros depending on how far over the limit you were travelling. The rental company is notified electronically if a camera catches you, and the fine is charged to your credit card.
montenegroBody.mountainP1: Montenegrin mountain roads are beautiful and demanding in equal measure. The Lovcen serpentine (Budva to Njeguski) has 25 hairpin bends, no guard rails on many sections, and a gradient that requires second gear for most of the ascent. The road to Durmitor via the Piva canyon is 170 km of continuous mountain driving with narrow bridges, unlit tunnels, and occasional rockfall debris.
montenegroBody.mountainP2: Practical advice: use low gears on descents to save your brakes. Honk before blind bends, it is standard practice and potentially lifesaving. Carry at least 2 litres of water per person. Keep headlights on even in daytime. Pull into passing places to let faster or larger vehicles through. And budget 30 percent more time than your GPS suggests.
montenegroBody.startLink: Start at {{link}}, the closest airport to the Budva Riviera.

borderCrossingBody.h1: Taking a Rental Car Across Balkan Borders
borderCrossingBody.p1: Montenegro shares land borders with five countries: Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Albania, Kosovo, and Serbia. Driving a rental car into any of them is permitted, provided you inform the rental company at booking so the paperwork is ready when you collect the keys.
borderCrossingBody.greenCardText: A Green Card (Carte Internationale d'Assurance Automobile) is a standardised document proving your vehicle carries valid third-party insurance in the country you are entering. Border guards request it at every Balkan checkpoint. Without one, you will be turned around.
borderCrossingBody.greenCard1: Typical cost: around 15 euros for a 15-day period
borderCrossingBody.greenCard2: Issued by: the rental company, at no hassle to you, just confirm the countries you plan to visit when booking
borderCrossingBody.greenCard3: Standard coverage: Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia. Kosovo may need to be added separately
borderCrossingBody.greenCardWarn: Before setting off, open the glovebox and verify the Green Card lists every country on your route. A card valid for Croatia alone will not get you into Bosnia. Five minutes of checking saves hours of frustration at the checkpoint.
borderCrossingBody.doc1: Passport or EU national ID card (must be original, not a photocopy)
borderCrossingBody.doc2: Driving licence, an international driving permit is recommended alongside non-Latin-script licences
borderCrossingBody.doc3: Original rental contract with company stamp (printed, not a screenshot on your phone)
borderCrossingBody.doc4: Green Card insurance certificate listing all destination countries
borderCrossingBody.doc5: Vehicle registration certificate (kept in the car by the rental company)
borderCrossingBody.croatiaText: The E65 coastal crossing between Herceg Novi and Dubrovnik is by far the most popular, and therefore the most congested. In July and August, two passport checks (Montenegrin exit, Croatian entry) combine with heavy tourist traffic to produce queues of one to two hours. The crossing itself is a simple road checkpoint with booths on both sides.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaTip: Timing matters enormously. Cross before 08:00 or after 20:00 and the wait drops to minutes. Weekday mornings are dramatically quieter than Saturday or Sunday. If you must cross mid-afternoon in August, bring water and patience.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaFerry: Heading north from Budva, the Kamenari, Lepetane car ferry crosses the narrowest point of the Riviera in about five minutes, saving a 45-minute drive around the inner shore via Tivat Airport. Ferries run continuously; the fare is a few euros.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaTitle: Into Albania: Sukobin or Hani i Hotit
borderCrossingBody.albaniaText: The coastal crossing at Sukobin/Muriqan, south of Ulcinj, is the more straightforward route, well-signed, with generally short queues. The inland alternative at Hani i Hotit, near the southern shore of Lake Skadar, is scenic but quieter and less familiar to foreign tourists.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaNote: Albanian road infrastructure is improving rapidly but remains unpredictable outside main highways. Speed limits drop on rural roads and surface quality can change without warning. Drive defensively, particularly at night.
borderCrossingBody.bosniaTitle: Into Bosnia: Scepan Polje or Vilusi
borderCrossingBody.bosniaText: Heading north from the coast, the Scepan Polje crossing near Foca accesses the Tara River gorge and Sutjeska National Park. From Niksic, the Vilusi crossing provides a route toward Trebinje and eventually Mostar. Both are lightly used and rarely involve waiting.
borderCrossingBody.serbiaText: The Dobrakovo checkpoint on the E65, north of Bijelo Polje, connects to the Serbian motorway network. From there, Belgrade is roughly four hours of straightforward motorway driving. Tolls are charged at motorway entry and exit booths, take a ticket when you join and pay based on distance when you leave.
borderCrossingBody.kosovoText: The Kula crossing near Rozaje leads to Peja/Pec in western Kosovo. Confirm with your rental company that Kosovo is explicitly listed on your Green Card, several insurers exclude it by default, and border guards will check.
borderCrossingBody.tip1: Arrange documents in a single envelope on the dashboard before joining the queue. Fumbling at the window wastes time
borderCrossingBody.tip2: Switch off the engine while stationary, guards notice and it puts them at ease
borderCrossingBody.tip3: Avoid the Debeli Brijeg crossing on Saturday and Sunday afternoons between June and September
borderCrossingBody.tip4: Inland crossings (Bosnia, Serbia, Kosovo) are almost always faster than the coastal Croatian one
borderCrossingBody.tip5: Carry euros in cash. Croatian motorway tolls accept cards, but Bosnian and Albanian fuel stations sometimes do not
borderCrossingBody.tip6: Check your mobile roaming plan before crossing. Data charges spike the moment your phone connects to a foreign network
borderCrossingBody.lawsTitle: Traffic Laws That Change at the Border
borderCrossingBody.lawsIntro: Regulations differ from one country to the next. The main surprises:
borderCrossingBody.law1: Croatia: daytime running lights mandatory at all times. Motorway tolls are collected by cash or card at barrier stations
borderCrossingBody.law2: Albania: lower speed limits on secondary roads than in Montenegro. Livestock on the carriageway is not uncommon outside towns
borderCrossingBody.law3: Bosnia: winter tyres compulsory from November through April. A first-aid kit and warning triangle must be in the vehicle
borderCrossingBody.law4: Serbia: absolute zero-tolerance alcohol limit. Motorways use toll booths, take a ticket on entry, pay on exit based on distance
borderCrossingBody.law5: Kosovo: right-hand traffic, same as Montenegro. An international driving permit is strongly recommended
borderCrossingBody.route1: Budva to Dubrovnik: 2 hours on the coastal E65, one border, and arguably the finest short coastal drive in the Mediterranean
borderCrossingBody.route2: Budva to Mostar: 3-4 hours via Trebinje. The Stari Most bridge and Ottoman quarter are the draw
borderCrossingBody.route3: Budva to Shkoder: roughly 3 hours through Podgorica and along Lake Skadar. A different country, a different pace entirely
borderCrossingBody.route4: Budva to Belgrade: 6-7 hours via Podgorica and the E65 north. Motorway most of the way on the Serbian side
borderCrossingBody.returnP: The return crossing is usually faster than the outbound one, especially at Debeli Brijeg. Montenegrin border officers are generally efficient and rarely hold up traffic. Make sure you still have all documents easily accessible, the Green Card, rental contract, and passport will be checked again. Refuel before crossing back into Montenegro if you have been driving in Croatia, as Croatian fuel prices tend to be slightly higher.
borderCrossingBody.ctaText: Looking for a car that comes with cross-border paperwork already sorted? Search our fleet, tick the countries you plan to visit at booking, and the Green Card will be in the glovebox when you collect the keys.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaFerryLink: Hiring from {{link}} is the most popular cross-border option.

blogIndex.heroSubtitle: Road routes, local secrets, and quiet corners of the outer Bay of Kotor — from people who drive these roads daily.
blogIndex.card_churches_title: Secret Churches and Chapels of Budva's Old Town
blogIndex.card_churches_excerpt: Beyond St Tryphon Cathedral, the Old Town hides a dozen smaller chapels tucked into back lanes and behind unmarked doorways. A walking route through the quietest sacred spaces.
blogIndex.card_vrmac_title: Driving and Hiking the Vrmac Ridge
blogIndex.card_vrmac_excerpt: The forested ridge between Budva and Tivat Airport offers panoramic views of both sides of the Riviera. Park at the trailhead and walk to the Austro-Hungarian fortress at the summit.
blogIndex.card_markets_title: Market Day in Budva: Fish, Cheese, and Wine
blogIndex.card_markets_excerpt: Where locals shop for fresh bay fish, Njeguski cheese, and Crmnica valley wine. A morning guide to the Green Market and waterfront stalls.
blogIndex.card_swimming_title: Best Swimming Spots Around the Bay
blogIndex.card_swimming_excerpt: From pebble beaches beneath fig trees to concrete platforms with ladders into deep blue water. Nine places to swim that most visitors never find.
blogIndex.card_cetinje_title: Budva to Cetinje via the Old Mountain Road
blogIndex.card_cetinje_excerpt: Skip the highway and take the original mountain road through Njeguski village and over the Lovcen shoulder. Smoked ham, monastery relics, and the former royal capital.
blogIndex.card_dobrota_title: Bečići: The Quiet Village Next to Budva
blogIndex.card_dobrota_excerpt: A 10-minute drive from the Old Town walls, Bečići's waterfront has stone palazzi, wine bars, and some of the Riviera's best sunset views.
blogIndex.card_kayak_title: Kayaking the Bay from the Coastal Road
blogIndex.card_kayak_excerpt: Where to launch a kayak around Budva Riviera, what to see from the water, and how to combine paddling with a driving day trip.
blogIndex.card_stoliv_title: Gornji Stoliv: Budva's Abandoned Cliff Village
blogIndex.card_stoliv_excerpt: A narrow road climbs 400 metres above the Riviera to a stone village abandoned in the 1979 earthquake. The views and the silence are extraordinary.
blogIndex.card_wildflowers_title: Spring Wildflower Drives: March to May
blogIndex.card_wildflowers_excerpt: The bay bursts into colour between March and May. Orchids, iris, and rockrose line the roads from Budva to Risan and up toward the Lovcen foothills.

blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.title: Bečići: The Quiet Waterfront Village Next to Budva
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.subtitle: A 10-minute drive from the Old Town walls, Bečići's stone promenade has palazzo ruins, wine bars, and the Riviera's best sunset views.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.description: Explore Bečići village near Budva Old Town. Waterfront walk, palazzo architecture, restaurants, and local wine bars on the Riviera.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.introP1: Bečići begins where Budva ends. Drive north from the Old Town along the Riviera road for ten minutes and the tourist crowds thin to nothing. The waterfront widens into a stone promenade lined with palazzo facades, some restored, some crumbling photogenically, and a scattering of restaurants and wine bars that Budva residents consider their own territory.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.introP2: The village stretches for about 2 km along the water's edge. There is no centre in the traditional sense, just a continuous ribbon of stone, water, and mountain. Park anywhere along the road (free outside summer peak) and walk.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.palazziP1: Bečići's wealthy sea captains built their homes along this stretch during the Venetian period. The Palazzo Tripkovic, Palazzo Dabinovic, and several others line the promenade with carved doorways, iron balconies, and walled gardens visible through rusted gates. Most are privately owned and not open to visitors, but the exteriors alone tell the story of a settlement that once rivalled Rafailovići in maritime wealth.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.palazziP2: Look for the stone carvings above doorways, many feature ships, anchors, or the Venetian lion, indicating the owner's profession and allegiance. The Palazzo Tripkovic has a particularly fine carved lintel showing a galleon under full sail. Several buildings show earthquake damage from 1979 that has never been fully repaired: cracked walls, missing balconies, and the occasional open room visible through a collapsed ceiling. These ruins are part of Bečići's character rather than a sign of neglect.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.altWaterfront: Stone waterfront promenade in Bečići with palazzo facades and bay views
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.churchesP1: Two churches punctuate the waterfront walk. The Church of St Matthew (Sveti Matej) dates from the 15th century and holds a collection of religious paintings attributed to local and Venetian artists. Further along, the Church of St Eustace (Sveti Eustahije) is the larger of the two, with a bell tower that serves as a landmark for boats approaching from across the Riviera.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.churchesP2: St Eustace is Bečići's most significant building. The treasury contains religious silver and gold pieces gifted by sea captains returning from successful voyages. Opening hours are irregular, try mid-morning on weekdays for the best chance of finding it open. A sacristan sometimes sits outside and will unlock the treasury room for interested visitors. No formal entry fee, but a donation of a few euros is appropriate.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.diningP1: Bečići's restaurants occupy converted ground floors of old stone houses, with tables extending onto the waterfront in summer. The cuisine is the same as Budva, grilled fish, black risotto, local salads, but the atmosphere is noticeably calmer and the prices slightly lower. Several wine bars have opened in recent years, pouring Montenegrin wines by the glass alongside small plates of cheese and cured meats.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.diningP2: Konoba Catovica, roughly midway along the waterfront, is a local favourite for grilled fish and house wine. The stone terrace sits at water level, and on still evenings you can hear the Riviera lapping against the steps. Their grilled bream with blitva costs about 14 euros. Further along toward Ljuta, Restoran Stari Mlini (Old Mills) sits beside an old watermill and offers a more refined menu, try the black risotto with cuttlefish ink. Main courses run 14 to 20 euros. Both restaurants are considerably less crowded than anything inside Budva's walls.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.swimmingP: Several concrete platforms and stone steps along the Bečići waterfront provide access to swimming. The water is deep, clean, and relatively warm from June through September. The platform near the Church of St Matthew is popular with locals in the late afternoon. There is no beach as such, this is ladder-and-platform swimming, but the water quality is excellent and the mountain backdrop makes up for the lack of sand. Bring water shoes for the rocky entry points.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.historyP: Bečići's wealth came from the sea. During the centuries of Venetian rule, its captains sailed trading ships across the Mediterranean and returned with money, goods, and architectural ambitions. By the 18th century, Bečići's fleet numbered over 30 merchant vessels, and the village had its own maritime school and insurance fund. The palazzi along the waterfront were their legacy, grand stone houses built to impress visitors arriving by boat. The maritime tradition is documented in Budva's Maritime Museum, a 20-minute drive away, where Bečići captains feature prominently in the collection.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.seasonalP1: Summer Bečići is the village at its liveliest. Restaurant terraces fill from early evening, swimmers splash off the stone platforms, and the waterfront promenade becomes a passeggiata route for locals escaping the Old Town crowds. July and August bring the warmest water and the longest evenings, but also the heaviest parking demand.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.seasonalP2: Spring and autumn are quieter and in many ways more rewarding. The palazzo facades look their best in the soft light of April, when wisteria and bougainvillea drape the garden walls. October brings golden evening light that sets the stone buildings glowing. In winter, Bečići is almost deserted, a few cafes stay open, the churches are unlocked, and you can walk the entire waterfront without seeing another person. The mountains across the Riviera collect snow, and the contrast of white peaks above dark water is striking.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.sunsetP: Bečići faces west across the Riviera, directly into the sunset. The mountains above Rafailovići and Risan turn orange and gold in the last hour of daylight, reflected in the still water. Any point along the promenade works, but the stretch between the two churches is particularly good, unobstructed views, a bench or low wall to sit on, and the quiet broken only by the occasional boat motor.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.gettingP1: From Budva Old Town, drive north on the Riviera road. Bečići begins almost immediately, the transition is seamless. On-street parking is available throughout the village. In July and August, arrive before 18:00 if you want a restaurant table for sunset.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.gettingP2: Alternatively, walk from Budva. The promenade from the northern walls to the first palazzi of Bečići is about 800 metres along the waterfront, a flat, easy walk that takes 10 to 12 minutes. This is a good option on cruise-ship days when parking near Budva is impossible. For another bay road walk, see our guide to
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.gettingLink: the spring wildflower drives around the Riviera

cookieBody.whatText: Cookies are small text files stored on your device when you visit a website. They help the site remember your preferences and understand how you use it.
cookieBody.essentialText: These are necessary for the website to function. They cannot be disabled.
cookieBody.sessionDesc: maintain your browsing session while you navigate the site
cookieBody.preferenceDesc: remember your language and location selections
cookieBody.thirdPartyText: Our car rental booking widget (provided by LocalRent) may set cookies to:
cookieBody.thirdParty2: Maintain your session during the booking process
cookieBody.thirdPartyNote: These cookies are set by LocalRent and are governed by their privacy policy.
cookieBody.managingText: You can control cookies through your browser settings. Most browsers allow you to:
cookieBody.managingNote: Note that blocking essential cookies may affect the functionality of the booking widget.
cookieBody.contactText: Questions about our use of cookies? Contact us at info@budvacarrental.com.

privacyBody.whoText: Car Rental Budva operates as an affiliate partner connecting travellers with car rental providers in and around Budva, Montenegro. Our website is located at budvacarrental.com. When you use our site, we may collect certain information as described below.
privacyBody.provideText: When you use our booking search form, we collect the pickup location, dates, and times you enter in order to find available vehicles. This information is passed to our partner (LocalRent) to generate search results. We do not collect or store payment details, all payments are processed directly by the rental provider.
privacyBody.auto1: pages visited, time spent on site, referring website
privacyBody.auto2: browser type, operating system, screen resolution
privacyBody.auto3: used for approximate location and security purposes
privacyBody.how1: To connect you with car rental availability and pricing
privacyBody.how2: To improve our website and user experience
privacyBody.how3: To understand how visitors use our site (analytics)
privacyBody.how4: To respond to enquiries via WhatsApp, Viber, or email
privacyBody.thirdText: Our website uses the following third-party services:
privacyBody.third1: car rental booking widget, processes your search queries and handles all booking and payment
privacyBody.thirdNote: Each of these services has their own privacy policy governing how they handle data.
privacyBody.cookiesText: We use minimal cookies necessary for the website to function. The LocalRent booking widget may set its own cookies for session management and preferences. See our {{link}} for details.
privacyBody.right1: Request access to any personal data we hold about you
privacyBody.right2: Request correction or deletion of your data
privacyBody.right4: Lodge a complaint with a data protection authority
privacyBody.contactText: For any privacy-related questions, contact us via email at info@budvacarrental.com.

termsBody.aboutText: Car Rental Budva (budvacarrental.com) is an affiliate website that connects travellers with car rental services in and around Budva, Montenegro. We are not a car rental company, we partner with LocalRent to provide real-time availability and booking services.
termsBody.bookingP1: All car rental bookings made through our website are processed and fulfilled by our partner, LocalRent. When you complete a booking, you enter into a rental agreement directly with the vehicle supplier, not with Car Rental Budva.
termsBody.bookingP2: The rental terms, including cancellation policies, insurance coverage, age requirements, and payment conditions, are set by the vehicle supplier and displayed during the booking process. We recommend reading these terms carefully before confirming any booking.
termsBody.provide1: A search interface to browse available rental vehicles
termsBody.provide2: Destination and travel information about Budva and the Budva Riviera region
termsBody.provide3: Customer support to help with enquiries before booking
termsBody.doNot1: Own, operate, or maintain any rental vehicles
termsBody.doNot3: Set rental prices, insurance terms, or cancellation policies
termsBody.pricingText: Prices displayed on our website are provided in real-time by our booking partner and are subject to change. The final price is confirmed at the time of booking. All prices include VAT and mandatory insurance unless otherwise stated.
termsBody.contentText: We make every effort to ensure that information on this website is accurate and up to date. However, travel information, driving regulations, and border crossing requirements can change. We recommend verifying critical details (especially border crossing documents and driving rules) with official sources before your trip.
termsBody.liabilityText: Car Rental Budva acts as an intermediary and is not liable for:
termsBody.liability1: The condition or availability of rental vehicles
termsBody.liability2: Actions or omissions by rental suppliers
termsBody.liability3: Losses arising from border crossing issues or documentation problems
termsBody.liability4: Changes to prices after booking confirmation
termsBody.ipText: All content on this website, including text, design, and graphics, is owned by Car Rental Budva unless otherwise credited. Stock photography is used under licence from Pexels.
termsBody.contactText: Questions about these terms? Contact us at info@budvacarrental.com.

affiliate.whoBloggersText: Write about Montenegro, the Balkans, or European road trips? Your readers are already looking for rental cars.
affiliate.whoLocalText: Hotels, apartments, tour operators, and transfer companies in Montenegro, recommend cars to your guests.
affiliate.whoCreatorsText: YouTube, Instagram, TikTok, share your Budva Riviera driving content and monetise with rental links.

popularRoutes.label: Popular Routes
popularRoutes.title: Scenic Drives from Herceg Novi
popularRoutes.subtitle: Four roads worth renting a car for.

0.title: Herceg Novi to Perast via Kamenari Ferry
0.desc: Magistrala to Kamenari, five-minute roll-on ferry, then the inner bay road to Perast and Our Lady of the Rocks.

1.title: Herceg Novi to Kotor
1.desc: Via the Kamenari–Lepetane ferry to save 40 minutes around the bay. UNESCO walls and the climb to San Giovanni await.

2.title: Herceg Novi to Ledenice on Orjen
2.desc: Switchback climb from the coast to 700 m on Orjen mountain. Windows open, second gear, and the outer bay spreading out behind you.

3.title: Herceg Novi to Dubrovnik
3.desc: Coastal E65 through Igalo, Debeli Brijeg border, Konavle valley. Green Card in the glovebox, passports on the dashboard.

insiderTips.label: Outer Bay Wisdom
insiderTips.title: Three Rules for the Outer Bay

0.title: Mimosa Festival Weekend Books Out
0.text: The February Mimosa Festival fills every hotel and rental in Herceg Novi for a single weekend. If you’re arriving in late Feb, book the car the same week you book the hotel — supply is thin.

1.title: The Žanjice Descent Is Brake-Light Country
1.text: The back road down to Žanjice and Mirišta on Luštica is steep, tight and full of blind corners. Second gear, windows open for echo, and take every pull-off offered by oncoming traffic.

2.title: Croatia in 40 Minutes
2.text: Debeli Brijeg is the closest Montenegro–Croatia crossing in the country — 40 minutes from the Herceg Novi waterfront. Green Card in the glovebox, passports on the dashboard, and you’re on the road to Dubrovnik inside the hour.

fleetIndex.title: Our Herceg Novi Rental Fleet
fleetIndex.subtitle: Seven cars picked for a multi-day Herceg Novi base, with day-trips out to Kotor, Dubrovnik, Orjen and across to Trebinje.
fleetIndex.seoDesc: Seven multi-day rental cars for Herceg Novi — mid-size wagons, diesel cruisers and a city Fiat. Specs, fuel range, and which car suits day-trips out of the outer bay.
fleetIndex.intro1: This fleet is built around the multi-day Budva renter — visitors who anchor themselves at a Bečići apartment or an Old Town guesthouse for a fortnight or longer, and who expect to be spending real time on the road between bay-based days. That brief is different from a cruise-day hire or a pure Tivat Airport Airport pickup: it leans on boot space for a weekly shop at Voli, diesel torque for the Sozina motorway run, and a seating position that keeps four adults comfortable on the 3-hour push to Žabljak. The seven cars below are chosen accordingly.
fleetIndex.intro2: The mix is deliberately weighted toward mid-size. A Peugeot 308 with the 1.5 BlueHDi and the EAT auto, a Renault Megane diesel with the segment’s biggest boot, a VW Golf with the DSG 2.0 TDI — these are the cars that turn a Budva → Cetinje → Lake Skadar → Sozina loop from a gear-changing chore into a cruise. For shorter hops we keep a Renault Clio, a Citroen C3 with Advanced Comfort dampers, a Kia Stonic crossover for the rutted spur roads up to Njeguši and Gornja Lastva, and a tiny Fiat 500 for when Muo waterfront parking is the deciding constraint. No one-trick supercars, no vans; every car here justifies a multi-day hire.
fleetIndex.intro3: A practical Budva note before you choose. Diesel remains the smarter spec for anyone planning even one cross-border run — Trebinje is 90 minutes, Dubrovnik via Debeli Brijeg is also 90 minutes, Mostar is a full day each way on a good cruise. The 308 and Megane will cover all three on a single tank, the Golf is the most refined motorway car we have, and the petrol Clio will still do a Budva → Dubrovnik return on half a tank. If your week is bay-contained — Rafailovići, Prčanj, Muo, Risan — the Fiat 500 or the C3 saves a third on weekly fuel and parks in places the rest of the fleet can’t. Pick on your itinerary, not the daily rate.
